words.english: two days before / two days after

true_relatives.note: नरसों likely derives from a further extension of the Sanskrit paraśvas system — it marks three days away from today in either direction

useful_coincidences.note: Russian has no single word for 'three days from now'; Hindi's compact नरसों is more expressive, making it worth memorising as a uniquely useful item

sound_alikes.note: 'narsõ' sounds close to 'narrows' — picture time narrowing down to a specific point three days away

evolution.meaning: three days before / three days after (= two days beyond परसों)

root_breakdown.logic: Hindi has a rare system of named relative days: आज (today) → कल (±1 day) → परसों (±2 days) → नरसों (±3 days). Each step goes one day further, with the same past/future ambiguity resolved by context.

etymology_story: Hindi preserves an ancient Sanskrit system of naming days relative to today, extending outward one step at a time.
Where परसों covers two days away, नरसों covers three days away — the next step in the same symmetrical system.
Like कल and परसों, नरसों is bidirectional: it means both three days ago and three days from now, with tense clarifying which.

pronunciation_guide.stress: roughly even; the final syllable has the same nasal quality as परसों

tips.tip: 'narsõ' sounds like 'narrows' with the final 'ws' replaced by a nasal 'õ'

memory_hook.primary: A calendar with three squares highlighted in each direction from today — the third square on each side glowing with the same colour, labelled with matching arrows pointing both ways
memory_hook.connection: The symmetrical three-square arrows encode the core logic of नरसों: three days out in either direction, with no inherent past/future bias

examples.hindi: नरसों मेरा जन्मदिन है।
examples.romanisation: narsõ merā janmadin hai.
examples.english: My birthday is three days from now.

examples.hindi: नरसों बहुत बड़ी बारिश हुई थी।
examples.romanisation: narsõ bahut baṛī bāriś huī thī.
examples.english: Three days ago there was very heavy rain.
examples.literal: three-days-ago very big rain happened was

examples.hindi: परसों नहीं, नरसों आना।
examples.romanisation: parsõ nahī̃, narsõ ānā.
examples.english: Not the day after tomorrow — come three days from now.

examples.hindi: नरसों से ट्रेनिंग शुरू होगी।
examples.romanisation: narsõ se ṭreiniṅg śurū hogī.
examples.english: Training will begin three days from now.

true_relatives.meaning: right now, immediately, at this very moment
true_relatives.note: Direct inheritance — अभी is unchanged from Sanskrit, built from अभि (towards, upon) + the present time marker

useful_coincidences.note: No ancestry connection, but the learner's Hebrew akhshav is the closest functional parallel — both mean 'right this moment'

sound_alikes.note: 'abhī' sounds like saying 'a bee' quickly — picture a bee buzzing right in front of your face right now

root.meaning: towards, upon, right at (a prefix of immediacy and presence)

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit prefix अभि means 'right at, towards' — combined with an emphatic particle, it created अभी meaning 'at this exact moment'. The immediacy of अभि is still felt in the word's urgency.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit prefix अभि (abhi) conveyed immediacy and presence — 'right at, towards, upon this very thing'.
Combined with an emphatic particle, it formed अभी — meaning 'at this very moment, right now'.
Modern Hindi preserves it unchanged, and अभी-अभी (reduplicated) intensifies the meaning to 'just this instant'.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the second syllable — ab·HĪ; the ī is long

tips.tip: 'abhī' sounds like saying 'a bee' — two syllables, stress on the second, same vowel quality

memory_hook.primary: A bee hovering directly in front of a person's face, wings blurring with speed, the person frozen mid-step — the moment captured in absolute stillness
memory_hook.connection: The bee right in your face right this instant encodes 'abhī' through sound, and the frozen immediate-present moment encodes the meaning 'right now'

examples.hindi: मैं अभी आता हूँ।
examples.romanisation: maĩ abhī ātā hū̃.
examples.english: I'm coming right now.

examples.hindi: वह अभी-अभी गया है।
examples.romanisation: vah abhī-abhī gayā hai.
examples.english: He has just left this very moment.

examples.hindi: अभी मेरे पास वक़्त नहीं है।
examples.romanisation: abhī mere pās vaqt nahī̃ hai.
examples.english: Right now I don't have time.
examples.literal: right-now my near time not is

examples.hindi: क्या तुम अभी घर पर हो?
examples.romanisation: kyā tum abhī ghar par ho?
examples.english: Are you at home right now?
examples.literal: what you right-now home on are

true_relatives.note: Both components are Sanskrit; भी is the Sanskrit emphatic/additive particle api, meaning 'also, even, too'

useful_coincidences.note: Hebrew adayin is the closest functional parallel — both express that a state which started in the past continues into the present
Russian vsyo yeshchyo has the same meaning — both are two-word phrases expressing continuation into the present

sound_alikes.note: 'abhī bhī' sounds like 'a-bee-bee' — picture a bee that is still there, still buzzing, refusing to leave

root_breakdown.logic: The phrase combines अभी (right now) with भी (also/even). Together they mean 'even right now' — stressing that a state continues into the present despite expectation that it might have ended.

etymology_story: अभी (right now) comes from the Sanskrit prefix abhi (immediacy), while भी comes from Sanskrit api (also, even).
The combination अभी भी emerged as a natural phrase to emphasise continuation — 'even now, at this very moment'.
It functions like English 'still' — implying slight surprise that something has not changed or ended.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the middle syllable (hī); भी receives secondary stress

tips.tip: the first two syllables 'abhī' sound like 'a bee', and then 'bhī' adds another 'bee' — 'a-bee-bee'

memory_hook.primary: A bee that was there an hour ago, still hovering in exactly the same spot, wings still blurring, not having moved at all — a frozen insistence on staying
memory_hook.connection: The bee from अभी (right now) is still there — अभी भी (still/even now) — the persistence of the bee encodes the meaning of continuation

examples.hindi: वह अभी भी दिल्ली में है।
examples.romanisation: vah abhī bhī dillī mẽ hai.
examples.english: He is still in Delhi.

examples.hindi: क्या तुम अभी भी सो रहे हो?
examples.romanisation: kyā tum abhī bhī so rahe ho?
examples.english: Are you still sleeping?
examples.literal: what you still sleep doing are

examples.hindi: मुझे अभी भी उस दिन याद है।
examples.romanisation: mujhe abhī bhī us din yād hai.
examples.english: I still remember that day.
examples.literal: me-to still that day memory is

examples.hindi: वह काम अभी भी नहीं हुआ।
examples.romanisation: vah kām abhī bhī nahī̃ huā.
examples.english: That work still hasn't been done.

true_relatives.meaning: now, at this time (a Sanskrit particle of present time)
true_relatives.note: अब descends from the Sanskrit particle ava, which indicated the present moment or current situation

useful_coincidences.note: Not useful for meaning, but 'av' / 'ab' are near-identical sounds across distant languages — the shortness of अब mirrors the shortness of the Hebrew equivalents

sound_alikes.note: 'ab' sounds like a short, punchy 'up' — both are crisp, immediate, forward-pointing words

root.meaning: down, off, away; also: now, at this point (in context)

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit particle evolved through Prakrit into अब, shedding its other meanings and settling firmly on 'now' — the present moment, the current state of affairs.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit particle ava had a range of uses, including indicating the current moment or current state.
Through Prakrit the form simplified and the meaning narrowed to 'now' — the present time.
Modern Hindi अब is one of the most basic time words, contrasting with तब (then) in the same way English 'now' contrasts with 'then'.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable; short 'a' as in 'cut', crisp 'b' at the end

tips.tip: 'ab' has the same crisp energy as 'up' — short, immediate, no trailing vowel

memory_hook.primary: A large clock with its hands pointing straight up to 12 — the NOW position — with the word AB printed across the clock face in bold letters
memory_hook.connection: The clock frozen at 12 (the universal 'right now') encodes the concept of present time, and the two letters AB on the face anchor the sound of अब

examples.hindi: अब हमें जाना चाहिए।
examples.romanisation: ab hamẽ jānā cāhie.
examples.english: Now we should go.

examples.hindi: अब तक कोई नहीं आया।
examples.romanisation: ab tak koī nahī̃ āyā.
examples.english: No one has come until now.

examples.hindi: अब से ऐसा मत करना।
examples.romanisation: ab se aisā mat karnā.
examples.english: Don't do this from now on.

examples.hindi: अब क्या करें?
examples.romanisation: ab kyā karẽ?
examples.english: Now what should we do?

true_relatives.note: तब descends from Sanskrit tadā — the demonstrative pronoun base tat (that) + the time suffix dā

useful_coincidences.note: तब and 'then' are functional mirrors — both contrast with 'now/अब' and both are used in if-then / when-then constructions

sound_alikes.note: 'तब' is pronounced exactly like the English word 'tab' — picture keeping a 'tab' on the past, tracking what happened 'then'

root.meaning: that (the demonstrative pronoun — referring to something away from the speaker)

root_breakdown.logic: तब is built on the Sanskrit demonstrative base tat (that) — so it literally means 'at that (time)'. It is the natural pair to अब (now) and to जब (when), forming the correlative जब … तब (when … then).

etymology_story: The Sanskrit demonstrative tat (that) combined with a time suffix to create tadā — 'at that time, then'.
Through Prakrit the form contracted to तब, shedding syllables while keeping its core pointing-to-the-past meaning.
In Hindi, तब pairs naturally with जब (when) in correlative sentences: जब मैं छोटा था, तब… (when I was small, then…).

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable; exactly like the English word 'tab'

tips.tip: 'तब' is pronounced identically to the English word 'tab' — same vowel, same consonants

memory_hook.primary: A dusty, yellowed calendar from years past lying open, a finger pointing at one faded date — capturing a specific moment frozen in the past
memory_hook.connection: The old calendar with a pointing finger at a past date is the visual for 'at that time' — तब always points to a moment away from now, just as a pointing finger on an old calendar does

examples.hindi: जब मैं बच्चा था, तब हम दिल्ली में रहते थे।
examples.romanisation: jab maĩ baccā thā, tab ham dillī mẽ rahte the.
examples.english: When I was a child, we lived in Delhi.
examples.literal: when I child was, then we Delhi in lived were

examples.hindi: तब तक मुझे इंतज़ार करना होगा।
examples.romanisation: tab tak mujhe intazār karnā hogā.
examples.english: I will have to wait until then.
examples.literal: then until me-to wait do will-be

examples.hindi: अब और तब में बहुत फ़र्क है।
examples.romanisation: ab aur tab mẽ bahut fark hai.
examples.english: There is a big difference between now and then.
examples.literal: now and then in very difference is

examples.hindi: तब से वह यहाँ नहीं आया।
examples.romanisation: tab se vah yahā̃ nahī̃ āyā.
examples.english: He hasn't come here since then.
examples.literal: then from he here not came
